prompts.text: Explain why people get nervous before talking in front of a group.
Explain to a younger sibling why saving money matters.
Explain why some friendships fade over time.
Explain why people argue about small things when they're tired.
Explain to a kid why apologies are hard.
Explain why some people enjoy being alone.
Explain how a habit forms, like checking your phone first thing in the morning.
Explain to your parents why your generation thinks about work differently than theirs.
Explain to a 12-year-old what 'compromise' really means.
Explain why feeling jealous of a friend isn't always a bad thing.
Explain how a person decides whether to trust a stranger.
Explain to a kid why adults sometimes lie to be polite.
Explain why moving to a new city can feel lonely even when it's exciting.
Explain what it means to 'set a boundary' to someone who's never heard the phrase.
Explain to your grandparents how online friendships actually work.
Explain why people stay in jobs they don't love.
Explain to a child what 'regret' feels like and where it comes from.
Explain to a 12-year-old how you decide what 'success' means for you.
Explain why some people forgive easily and others can't.
Explain to a kid why grown-ups sometimes cry at things that aren't sad.
Tell us the most important thing you learned last year.
Tell us your honest opinion on whether people should live with roommates.
Tell us whether you'd rather be respected or liked.
Tell us if you think small talk is worth doing.
Tell us whether you'd move across the country for a job you weren't sure about.
Tell us your verdict on whether to text or call a friend you haven't seen in a year.
Tell us what you actually think about giving unsolicited advice.
Tell us how a recent disagreement with someone close to you ended.
Tell us whether the friend group you have now is the one you'll have in ten years.
Tell a friend who's deciding between two job offers what you'd do.
Tell us the one habit you'd quit today if you could.
Tell us your stance on whether being busy is a status symbol.
Tell us if you'd rather have a boring stable life or an exciting unstable one.
Tell your parents in one sentence why you made a big life choice they disagreed with.
Tell us what changed your mind about something you used to feel strongly about.
Tell us your conclusion after a year of trying something new.
Tell us the real reason you stopped talking to someone.
Tell us what you'd say if a younger version of you asked whether you turned out okay.
Tell us, in one sentence, what you'd do differently if you could redo your twenties.
Tell us the hardest truth you've had to accept about yourself.
Describe what a long friendship feels like using one everyday thing.
Compare being in your twenties to one ordinary object around your house.
Describe what trust feels like using one everyday experience.
Compare a bad first impression to one common moment in daily life.
Describe what waiting for someone's reply feels like using one everyday thing.
Compare regret to one specific thing in your kitchen.
Compare burnout to one ordinary household experience.
Describe what it feels like to outgrow a friendship using one everyday object.
Compare giving feedback to a friend to one everyday physical task.
Describe a long-distance relationship using one everyday thing.
Compare being the new person at a party to one mundane moment.
Describe what it's like to forgive someone using one everyday activity.
Compare making a major life decision to one everyday cooking task.
Describe what 'starting over' feels like using one ordinary moment from your week.
Compare procrastination to one common thing kids do.
Describe what loneliness in a crowd feels like using one everyday object.
Compare losing touch with an old friend to one everyday seasonal change.
Describe what 'finding yourself' actually means using one specific everyday thing.
Compare a hard conversation you've been avoiding to one ordinary chore.
Describe what change feels like in your thirties using one everyday object.
Describe what you do at work to a curious neighbor who knows nothing about your field.
Explain a hobby you love to someone who's never tried it, without using any insider words.
Describe the last show or book you loved without using fan vocabulary.
Explain a sport you follow to someone who's never watched it.
Describe your morning routine to a stranger, avoiding any trendy wellness words.
Explain what 'self-care' actually means without using any internet-flavored phrases.
Describe what therapy is to someone who's skeptical, without using therapy words.
Explain a recent argument you had without using any of the buzzwords people use online to describe relationship dynamics.
Describe your dream weekend to your grandparents without using any phrases they wouldn't recognize.
Explain how dating works now to an older relative, avoiding all app-specific vocabulary.
Describe a personality type you find difficult, without using horoscope or personality-test labels.
Explain what 'work-life balance' means to you without using the phrase itself.
Describe a friend's career to your parents without any industry shorthand.
Explain what makes a relationship 'healthy' without using any pop-psychology terms.
Describe a recent emotion you couldn't shake, without using any clinical or trendy mental-health labels.
Explain why you set a recent boundary with a family member, without using the words 'boundary,' 'toxic,' or 'energy.'
Describe what it means to 'grow as a person' without using any self-help vocabulary.
Explain why you feel disconnected from someone close to you, using only plain everyday language.
Describe your own personality to a stranger, with no labels, types, or trendy adjectives allowed.
Explain to your parents why you're rethinking a big life path, without any of the language your friends would use.
Tell us about a time a small kindness from a stranger stuck with you.
Describe the best meal you've had in the last year and why it mattered.
Tell us about a moment recently when you felt genuinely proud of yourself.
Describe a time you misjudged someone at first.
Tell us about a place you keep coming back to and what it looks like.
Describe a recent moment you laughed harder than you expected.
Tell us why your closest friend is your closest friend, with specifics, not adjectives.
Describe a time you changed your mind about a person.
Tell us about a habit you broke and what actually changed.
Describe the moment you knew a relationship was over — not the breakup, the moment.
Tell us about a piece of advice that actually worked, and how you used it.
Describe a time you stood up for yourself and what you actually said.
Tell us about the last time you cried and what set it off.
Describe a small daily ritual you have and the exact reason you do it.
Tell us about a time you were wrong about yourself.
Describe the most recent compliment that meant something to you and why.
Tell us about a turning point in your life and the specific day it happened.
Describe a time you felt out of place and what tipped you off.
Tell us about a moment you understood your parents for the first time.
Describe a decision you regret, with the actual details of the day you made it.
What's the single most important thing in a friendship?
What's the one quality you most want in a partner?
What's the one thing you wish your parents had taught you?
What's the one habit that has shaped you most?
What's the single best piece of advice you've ever gotten?
What's the one thing you'd never compromise on?
What's the single biggest reason you'd move to a new city?
What's the one belief you hold that most of your friends don't share?
What's the single biggest thing that makes a long-term relationship work?
What's the one thing that would make your daily life noticeably better?
What's the single most overrated thing your generation values?
What's the one thing you'd want a stranger to know about you in 30 seconds?
What's the single biggest reason people stay in situations they shouldn't?
What's the one thing you've changed your mind about in the last five years?
What's the single most important question to ask before making a big life decision?
What's the one thing about adulthood no one prepared you for?
What's the single biggest reason a person should trust their gut?
What's the one thing that would have to be true for you to feel your life worked out?
What's the single biggest difference between who you are now and who you were five years ago?
What's the one thing you'd tell a younger person about regret?
A friend says, 'I don't get why you're upset about this.' Answer what they actually want to know.
Someone says, 'Why would anyone live alone if they could live with friends?' Answer the real confusion.
A coworker asks, 'Why are you taking that vacation?' Answer the question behind the question.
A friend asks, 'Why do you keep going to that same place?' Answer what they're really curious about.
Your parent asks, 'Why are you single?' Answer the actual confusion, not the surface question.
A kid asks, 'Why do adults complain about Mondays?' Answer what they actually want to know.
A friend says, 'I don't understand why you ended that friendship.' Answer the real confusion.
A stranger asks, 'Why did you move here?' Answer what they actually want to understand.
Someone asks, 'Why don't you just call your mom more often?' Answer the question underneath.
A friend says, 'I don't get why you don't drink.' Answer what they're really asking.
A relative asks, 'Why is your generation so anxious?' Answer the actual confusion.
A friend asks, 'Why do you keep dating people like that?' Answer what they really want to know.
Someone asks, 'Why don't you just say what you mean?' Answer the real question.
A friend says, 'Why don't you want kids?' Answer the underlying confusion, not the literal question.
Someone says, 'I don't understand why that hurt you.' Answer what they're missing.
A coworker asks, 'Why don't you push for a promotion?' Answer what they actually don't get.
A close friend says, 'I don't understand why you forgave them.' Answer the real confusion.
A parent asks, 'Why aren't you happy yet?' Answer what they're really asking.
A friend says, 'I don't get why you stayed so long.' Answer the actual question underneath.
Someone says, 'I don't understand how you can still talk to them.' Answer the real confusion.
Claim something about your best friend, and back it up in the same breath.
Make a claim about your hometown, and prove it without pausing.
Claim you're a good listener, and prove it in the same answer.
Make a claim about a habit you have, and back it with evidence.
Claim something about your family, and prove it in the same breath.
Make a claim about the weather where you live, and prove it with specifics.
Claim you've grown in the last year, and prove it with a specific change.
Make a claim about modern dating, and back it up with something you've actually seen.
Claim that your generation is misunderstood, and prove it with a concrete example.
Make a claim about what it takes to be a good friend, and prove it with a moment from your life.
Claim something about long-distance relationships, and back it with evidence.
Make a claim about how your parents raised you, and prove it with something specific.
Claim that one habit changed your life, and prove it with a measurable shift.
Make a claim about a city you've visited, and prove it with a moment from the trip.
Claim something true about marriage today, and back it up with something you've actually witnessed.
Make a claim about the kind of friend you are, and prove it with a recent example.
Claim something true about people who change careers in their thirties, and back it with what you've seen.
Make a claim about what 'real love' looks like, and prove it with a specific moment, not a general feeling.
Claim something about how grief actually works, and back it with something you or someone you know lived through.
Make a claim about the role social media plays in friendships, and prove it with a real example from your life.

In 30 words or fewer, explain how you make a hard decision.
In 30 words or fewer, describe a place that feels like home.
In 30 words or fewer, share a belief you'd defend to a stranger.
In 30 words or fewer, tell us what you'd do with an unexpected free week.
In 30 words or fewer, describe the thing you most want to be known for.
In 30 words or fewer, explain how you handle being wrong.
In 30 words or fewer, describe what success will look like for you in ten years.
In 30 words or fewer, explain the hardest lesson you've learned about people.
In 30 words or fewer, tell us what you'd say to yourself ten years ago.
